nodes.label: AI as writing assistant
nodes.label_negative: AI as story performer replacing human teller
nodes.grounding_span: I use AI models to help me with this. I provide the basic elements...and the model creates a story

nodes.label: Human control over story content and adaptation
nodes.label_negative: AI dictating story content without human input
nodes.grounding_span: I review it for overall tone and message. Typically, I will make a few changes

nodes.label: Human expertise and credibility in storytelling
AI responsiveness to iterative adjustments
nodes.grounding_span: I don't recall ever needing more than three turns with the model to get what I needed

nodes.label: Personalized classroom context integration
nodes.grounding_span: I always try to make the story reflect something we are doing in class

nodes.label: Student engagement and learning
nodes.grounding_span: The kids really got into the story. They loved the characters' names and talked about the story

nodes.label: Human connection and relationship
nodes.grounding_span: They crave human interaction. That is what storytelling is all about

nodes.label: Creative control and originality
nodes.grounding_span: I always try to make the story reflect something we are doing in class, some of the personalities of the students

nodes.label: Resistance to AI taking over storytelling
nodes.grounding_span: I've done this many times, always to good effect

nodes.label: Resistance to AI taking over storytelling
nodes.grounding_span: I'm not ready to let them 'take over'

nodes.label: Openness to using AI for math in future
nodes.grounding_span: I have not used it yet for math and this seems like an area where the models could be very helpful

nodes.label: Iterative refinement through revision requests
nodes.grounding_span: I repeat this process until I feel like I have something I can use

nodes.label: Context-driven audience adaptation
nodes.grounding_span: I can make adjustments in real time
